stem: A patient presents with altered mental status. Using the mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS, which of the following is NOT a common cause?

choices.feedback: U = Uremia is included in AEIOU-TIPS and is a common metabolic cause of AMS.

Correct! AEIOU-TIPS: A=Alcohol/Acidosis, E=Endocrine/Electrolytes/Encephalopathy, I=Infection (meningitis, UTI, sepsis), O=Overdose/Oxygen (hypoxia), U=Uremia. T=Trauma/Temperature, I=Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia), P=Psychiatric/Poisoning, S=Stroke/Seizure/Syncope. Asthma is a respiratory condition, not typically categorized in AMS differential. The systematic approach ensures no reversible cause is missed.
T = Trauma (subdural hematoma, epidural, TBI) is a key cause of AMS.
I = Infection is a critical cause of AMS, especially UTI in elderly, meningitis, sepsis.

stem: An elderly patient with baseline dementia is found to be acutely more confused. UA shows pyuria. What is the most important next step?

choices.text: Perform a systematic evaluation for all reversible causes before attributing AMS to UTI alone
choices.feedback: Correct! UTI is overdiagnosed as the cause of AMS in elderly. Asymptomatic bacteriuria is present in 25-50% of nursing home residents. Always evaluate: medication changes (anticholinergics, opioids, benzodiazepines), metabolic (glucose, Na, Ca, TSH), infectious (blood cultures, CXR, UA), neurologic (stroke, seizure), cardiac (MI can present as confusion in elderly). Treat UTI if present, but don't stop the workup there.

choices.text: Restrain the patient and administer haloperidol
choices.feedback: Chemical/physical restraints should be avoided as first-line. Identifying and treating the underlying cause is the priority.
CT head may be indicated but should be part of a systematic evaluation, not the sole next step. The question asks about the approach to AMS.

choices.text: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for UTI immediately
choices.feedback: Asymptomatic bacteriuria is common in elderly — pyuria alone may not explain AMS. A broader workup is needed.

stem: What bedside test should be performed on ALL patients presenting with altered mental status?

choices.feedback: Correct! Fingerstick glucose is the single most important immediate bedside test for AMS. Hypoglycemia is rapidly reversible and can present identically to stroke, intoxication, or psychosis. Delays in recognition → irreversible brain injury. If glucose <60: give D50W IV immediately. Other immediate bedside assessments: vital signs (fever, hypotension, hypoxia), pupil exam, GCS/mental status, fingerstick glucose, pulse oximetry.
LP is indicated if meningitis/encephalitis is suspected but is not performed on ALL AMS patients as an initial step.
CT head is important when indicated (focal deficits, trauma, anticoagulation) but is not a universal bedside test.
Tox screen is useful but results take time and do not change immediate management as quickly as glucose correction.

stem: A 55-year-old man presents with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm, diaphoresis, and nausea. ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. What is the diagnosis and affected coronary artery?

choices.feedback: Correct! ST elevation in II, III, aVF = inferior wall STEMI. The RCA supplies the inferior wall in ~85% of patients (right-dominant circulation). Always check right-sided leads (V4R) for RV involvement. RV infarction: avoid nitroglycerin and diuretics (preload-dependent). Treatment: emergent PCI (door-to-balloon <90 min) or fibrinolytics if PCI unavailable within 120 min. Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor + anticoagulation.
Posterior STEMI shows ST depression in V1-V3 (reciprocal changes) and ST elevation in posterior leads V7-V9. The leads described are inferior.
Lateral STEMI shows ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6 and involves the left circumflex artery. Inferior leads point to RCA.
Anterior STEMI affects leads V1-V4 and involves the LAD. Inferior leads (II, III, aVF) indicate a different territory.

stem: Which chest pain feature is MOST concerning for aortic dissection rather than ACS?

choices.text: Sudden-onset, severe, tearing/ripping pain radiating to the back with blood pressure differential between arms
choices.feedback: Correct! Aortic dissection red flags: sudden onset of maximal-intensity pain (vs. crescendo pattern in ACS), tearing/ripping quality, radiation to back (Type A: ascending → anterior chest; Type B: descending → back), BP differential >20 mmHg between arms, new aortic regurgitation murmur, pulse deficits, widened mediastinum on CXR. CT angiography is diagnostic. CRITICAL: thrombolytics (given for STEMI) are LETHAL in aortic dissection — always consider dissection before administering.
Troponin elevation suggests myocardial injury (ACS). Dissection can occasionally cause troponin elevation if it occludes a coronary ostium, but this is not a distinguishing feature.
Exertional pain is more suggestive of angina/ACS than dissection.
Nitroglycerin relief suggests angina. Dissection pain is not relieved by nitroglycerin.

stem: A patient presents with pleuritic chest pain that improves with sitting forward. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Takotsubo can mimic STEMI with ST elevation and troponin rise but typically shows apical ballooning on echo, not the PR depression pattern of pericarditis.
STEMI shows ST elevation in a specific coronary territory with reciprocal depression. Diffuse ST elevation is not ACS.
PE pain is pleuritic but ECG shows sinus tachycardia, right heart strain (S1Q3T3, RBBB), not diffuse ST elevation with PR depression.

Correct! Pericarditis classic findings: sharp/pleuritic chest pain worse supine, better sitting forward; diffuse ST elevation (concave up) with PR depression in all leads except aVR (PR elevation in aVR); friction rub on auscultation. Causes: viral (most common), uremia, post-MI (Dressler syndrome), autoimmune, malignancy. Treatment: NSAIDs + colchicine (reduces recurrence by 50%). Avoid anticoagulation if effusion present (hemorrhagic conversion risk). Echocardiogram to evaluate for effusion/tamponade.

stem: A patient has fever > 38.3°C for more than 3 weeks with no diagnosis after 1 week of inpatient workup. What are the three classic categories of FUO?

choices.feedback: This is a temporal classification of infections, not the FUO diagnostic framework.

choices.text: Nosocomial, drug fever, and factitious fever
choices.feedback: These are causes of fever in hospitalized patients but represent specific subtypes, not the three classic FUO categories.

Correct! Classic FUO categories: Infections (~30%): TB, endocarditis, abscess, osteomyelitis, CMV/EBV. Malignancies (~20%): lymphoma (most common), leukemia, renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma. Autoimmune/inflammatory (~20%): adult-onset Still disease, GCA/PMR, SLE, vasculitis, sarcoidosis. Undiagnosed (~30%): many resolve spontaneously. Workup: repeat cultures, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, echocardiogram, ESR/CRP/ferritin, peripheral smear, LDH, consider PET-CT if standard workup negative.
FUO categories are broader than just different infection types.

stem: Which malignancy is the most common cause of FUO?

choices.feedback: Correct! Lymphoma (especially non-Hodgkin lymphoma) is the most common malignancy causing FUO. Classic B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) may precede lymphadenopathy. CT or PET-CT often reveals occult lymphadenopathy or extranodal disease. Other malignancies in FUO: renal cell carcinoma (paraneoplastic fever), hepatocellular carcinoma, atrial myxoma (mimics endocarditis). Markedly elevated LDH, ferritin, or ESR should raise suspicion for malignancy.
Melanoma is typically diagnosed by skin examination. It is not a classic cause of prolonged FUO.
Lung cancer causes fever but is usually identified on chest imaging early in FUO workup.
Colon cancer can cause low-grade fever but is not the most common malignancy causing FUO.

stem: Drug fever should be suspected in FUO when which pattern is observed?

choices.text: High fever with rigors occurring only during drug infusion
choices.feedback: Infusion-related reactions occur during administration. Drug fever is sustained and present even between doses.

choices.text: Fever only during the night with normal daytime temperatures
choices.feedback: This pattern is not specific to drug fever. Nocturnal fevers can occur with many infections and malignancies.

choices.text: Relatively well-appearing patient with fever, eosinophilia, and temporal correlation with drug initiation
choices.feedback: Correct! Drug fever clues: patient looks better than expected for degree of fever, relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), eosinophilia (~25% of cases), rash (variable), temporal correlation with drug start (usually 7-10 days after initiation but can be delayed). Common culprits: antibiotics (beta-lactams, sulfonamides), anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), allopurinol. Diagnosis of exclusion — resolves within 48-72h of stopping offending drug.

choices.text: Fever resolving immediately after stopping the suspected drug
choices.feedback: Drug fever may take 48-72 hours to resolve after drug discontinuation, not immediate resolution.

stem: The Glasgow Coma Scale component for best verbal response scores:

choices.feedback: Verbal response is scored 1-5.

Correct! GCS = Eye (1-4) + Verbal (1-5) + Motor (1-6) = 3-15. Verbal: 5=oriented, 4=confused speech, 3=inappropriate words, 2=incomprehensible sounds, 1=none. Motor: 6=obeys commands, 5=localizes pain, 4=withdrawal, 3=abnormal flexion (decorticate), 2=extension (decerebrate), 1=none. GCS ≤8 = intubation indicated.
1-6 is the motor component scoring range.
1-3 is too narrow; this isn't any GCS component.

explanation:
GCS verbal: oriented=5, confused=4, inappropriate words=3, incomprehensible=2, none=1. Total GCS 3-15. GCS ≤8 = intubate.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK513298] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513298/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Fever of unknown origin (FUO) is defined as:

choices.text: Any fever lasting more than 1 week
choices.feedback: One week is too short for the classic FUO definition.

choices.text: Fever >38.3°C on several occasions, lasting >3 weeks, with diagnosis uncertain after 1 week of investigation
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic Petersdorf criteria (modified): fever >38.3°C, duration >3 weeks, uncertain diagnosis after appropriate investigation. Major categories: infections (~30%), malignancy (~20%), autoimmune/inflammatory (~15%), miscellaneous (~15%), undiagnosed (~20%). Most common infectious cause in developed countries: abscess, endocarditis, TB. Most common malignancy: lymphoma.
FUO includes cases with positive cultures if the source remains unidentified.
Post-operative fever has its own differential and criteria.

explanation:
FUO: fever >38.3°C for >3 weeks, undiagnosed after 1 week investigation. Causes: infection ~30% (abscess, endocarditis, TB), malignancy ~20% (lymphoma), autoimmune ~15%.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK532265] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532265/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Which malignancy is the most common cause of FUO?

choices.feedback: HCC can cause fever but is not the most common malignancy causing FUO.

Correct! Lymphoma (especially non-Hodgkin's) is the most common malignancy causing FUO. B symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss >10% in 6 months. Hodgkin lymphoma: Pel-Ebstein fever (periodic). Other malignancies: renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, leukemia, atrial myxoma. CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET scan are key investigations.
Lung cancer more commonly causes paraneoplastic syndromes than FUO.
Prostate cancer rarely causes FUO.

explanation:
Lymphoma: most common malignancy causing FUO. B symptoms: fever, night sweats, >10% weight loss. Pel-Ebstein fever in Hodgkin's. Others: RCC, HCC, leukemia, atrial myxoma.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK532265] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532265/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 75-year-old with dementia is found to be acutely more confused, with fluctuating attention and visual hallucinations. This presentation is most consistent with:

choices.feedback: Correct! Key features distinguishing delirium from dementia: ACUTE onset, FLUCTUATING attention, altered level of consciousness, visual hallucinations. Delirium often has an identifiable precipitant (infection, medication, metabolic).
Dementia has gradual onset/progression. Acute fluctuating confusion suggests superimposed delirium.
While LBD has visual hallucinations and fluctuations, acute change in baseline should first be evaluated as delirium.
New-onset psychosis in an elderly patient with dementia is delirium until proven otherwise.

explanation:
Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention and awareness, often superimposed on pre-existing dementia (the strongest risk factor). Assessment: CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) — requires acute onset + fluctuating course + inattention + EITHER disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness. Common precipitants: infection (UTI, pneumonia), medications (anticholinergics, opioids, benzodiazepines), metabolic (electrolytes, glucose, thyroid, liver, kidney), pain, urinary retention, constipation, sleep deprivation. Management: treat underlying cause, reorientation, minimize tethers, avoid anticholinergics/benzodiazepines, low-dose antipsychotics only if severe agitation poses safety risk.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK441923] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441923/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Fever of unknown origin (FUO) is classically defined as:

choices.text: Temperature >38.3°C on several occasions over ≥3 weeks with no diagnosis after appropriate initial evaluation
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic FUO definition (Petersdorf & Beeson): fever >38.3°C (101°F) on multiple occasions, lasting >3 weeks, with no diagnosis after 1 week of inpatient investigation (or 3 outpatient visits).

choices.text: Any fever lasting >48 hours in a hospitalized patient
choices.feedback: This describes hospital-acquired fever, not FUO.
If an infection is documented, FUO criteria are not met (a diagnosis exists).
Temperature height and short duration do not define FUO.

explanation:
Classical FUO: temperature >38.3°C on multiple occasions, duration >3 weeks, no diagnosis after appropriate workup. Major categories: infections (~30%), malignancy (~20%), autoimmune/inflammatory (~20%), miscellaneous (~15%), undiagnosed (~15%). Common infectious causes: TB (always consider), endocarditis, abscess. Common malignancies: lymphoma, renal cell carcinoma. Common autoimmune: adult-onset Still disease (quotidian fevers, salmon-colored rash, ferritin >10,000), temporal arteritis (>65, ESR elevated), SLE. Workup: blood cultures, CRP/ESR, peripheral smear, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, echo, FDG-PET/CT (increasingly used as next-line imaging for localizing inflammation/malignancy).

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK532265] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532265/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: In the initial evaluation of altered mental status, what must be assessed immediately?

choices.feedback: Imaging is important but comes after initial stabilization and rapid bedside assessments.

choices.text: Airway, breathing, circulation, fingerstick glucose, and vital signs
choices.feedback: Correct! ABCs first, then immediate bedside glucose (hypoglycemia is the most rapidly reversible cause of AMS). Check vitals (fever → infection; hypo/hypertension; oxygen saturation). Rapid neurologic exam: GCS, pupils, focal deficits, signs of trauma. Administer naloxone if opioid overdose suspected (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression). Think "AEIOU-TIPS" for differential.
LP may be needed but is not the immediate first step. Stabilization and glucose check come first.
Lab work is important but takes time. Bedside glucose and vitals can be assessed in seconds.

explanation:
ABCs first, then immediate bedside glucose (hypoglycemia is the most rapidly reversible cause of AMS). Check vitals (fever → infection; hypo/hypertension; oxygen saturation). Rapid neurologic exam: GCS, pupils, focal deficits, signs of trauma. Administer naloxone if opioid overdose suspected (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression). Think "AEIOU-TIPS" for differential.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK441973] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441973/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: The mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS for altered mental status stands for:

choices.text: Alcohol, Epilepsy, Insulin, Overdose, Uremia, Trauma, Infection, Psychiatric, Stroke
choices.feedback: Correct! A: Alcohol/Acidosis. E: Epilepsy/Endocrine/Encephalopathy. I: Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia). O: Overdose/Oxygen (hypoxia). U: Uremia. T: Trauma/Temperature. I: Infection (meningitis, sepsis, UTI in elderly). P: Psychiatric/Poisoning. S: Stroke/Seizure/Shock. Systematic approach ensures no reversible cause is missed.

choices.text: Anemia, Embolism, Ischemia, Obstruction, Ulcer, Tachycardia, Infarction, Pericarditis, Syncope
choices.feedback: This is not the correct expansion. AEIOU-TIPS is specific to altered mental status differential diagnosis.

choices.text: Atrial fibrillation, Edema, Infection, Orthopnea, Urticaria, Thrombosis, Infarction, Pneumonia, Sepsis
choices.feedback: This is not the correct expansion for AEIOU-TIPS.

choices.text: Appendicitis, Ectopic, Ileus, Ovarian torsion, UTI, Trauma, Intussusception, PID, SBO
choices.feedback: This list relates to abdominal pain differential, not altered mental status.

explanation:
A: Alcohol/Acidosis. E: Epilepsy/Endocrine/Encephalopathy. I: Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia). O: Overdose/Oxygen (hypoxia). U: Uremia. T: Trauma/Temperature. I: Infection (meningitis, sepsis, UTI in elderly). P: Psychiatric/Poisoning. S: Stroke/Seizure/Shock. Systematic approach ensures no reversible cause is missed.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK441973] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441973/
✅ Verified: 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: In an elderly patient with acute confusion, which diagnosis must be considered even without typical infectious symptoms?

choices.feedback: Correct! UTI is a very common cause of acute delirium in elderly patients, often presenting WITHOUT classic urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency). Elderly patients may present only with altered mental status, falls, or decreased oral intake. Always check urinalysis in elderly patients with new confusion. Other common causes: medications (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids), dehydration, constipation, and metabolic derangements.
Appendicitis typically presents with abdominal pain, not isolated confusion.
While PE can cause confusion via hypoxia, UTI is a far more common cause of isolated AMS in the elderly.
Aortic dissection presents with severe chest/back pain, not typically isolated confusion.

explanation:
UTI is a very common cause of acute delirium in elderly patients, often presenting WITHOUT classic urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency). Elderly patients may present only with altered mental status, falls, or decreased oral intake. Always check urinalysis in elderly patients with new confusion. Other common causes: medications (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids), dehydration, constipation, and metabolic derangements.

📖 Reference: StatPearls [NBK441973] — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441973/
✅ Verified: 2025
